title: Trace Order Lines Across Four Tables
subtitle: Add one join at a time and finish at line-item grain
learning_objective: Follow customer, order, line-item, and product relationships without joining unrelated keys.
why: Transactional models split descriptive and detailed data across several normalized tables.
prompt: Return `order_id`, `customer_name`, `product_name`, `quantity`, and `line_total` for order lines whose order has a known customer. Calculate `line_total` as quantity × unit price. Order by `order_id`, then `product_id`.
expected_result: 8 rows. The orphan order 1007 is excluded because no customer matches; order 1001 appears twice because it has two lines.
build_steps: Calculate each line total and order by order and product.
explanation: The final grain is one order item because `order_items` is the most detailed table in the chain.
common_mistakes: Joining `order_id` to `product_id` because both are integers.
Expecting one row per order after selecting line details.
hints: The path is orders → customers and orders → order_items → products.
takeaways: Multi-table joins are a sequence of individual relationships.
The most detailed selected table determines result grain.
stretch_goal: Filter the result to completed orders only.
starter_sql:
SELECT
    o.order_id,
    c.customer_name,
    p.product_name,
    oi.quantity,
    oi.quantity * oi.unit_price AS line_total
FROM orders AS o
INNER JOIN customers AS c
    ON -- customer relationship
INNER JOIN order_items AS oi
    ON -- order relationship
INNER JOIN products AS p
    ON -- product relationship
ORDER BY o.order_id, p.product_id;
solution_explanation: Each join follows one foreign-key relationship, and the selected line-item columns establish one row per order item.
solution_walkthrough: Orders connect to customers through customer_id.
Orders connect to order_items through order_id.
Order items connect to products through product_id.
Line totals are calculated at the final detail grain.
